did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning approach combining YOLOv5 (an object detection model) for skin lesion localization and Support Vector Machine (SVM) for post-classification of skin lesions. The model was trained and tested on the ISIC 2018 and HAM10000 datasets to detect melanoma, nevus, and basal cell carcinoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a hybrid YOLOv5-SVM model for skin lesion detection. It details specific methods (preprocessing, data augmentation, training on ISIC-2018 and HAM10000 datasets), reports new quantitative results (96.8% accuracy, F1-score 0.93, AUC 0.981), and includes ablation studies and comparative analysis against baseline models. Although published in a conference proceedings (HSWTech 2025), it contains full original research content rather than being a review or abstract.

title: YOLOv5 with SVM-Aided Post-Classification for High-Precision Skin Lesion Detection
journal: 2025 International Conference on Sustainable Technologies for Humanity and Smart World (HSWTech)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning architecture combining YOLOv5 for real-time skin lesion localization with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for post-classification to improve diagnostic precision. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2018 and HAM10000 datasets, utilizing a balanced split of 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% testing data. Preprocessing steps included contrast enhancement, histogram equalization, and data augmentation to address class imbalance and enhance feature representation. Experimental results demonstrated that the YOLOv5-SVM model achieved a classification accuracy of 96.8%, an F1-score of 0.93, an AUC of 0.981, and an IoU of 0.86. Comparative analysis showed that this hybrid approach outperformed baseline models such as standalone YOLOv5, ResNet, and standard CNN classifiers. The findings suggest that integrating object detection with SVM-based post-classification effectively reduces false positives and enhances the reliability of AI-powered dermatological screening tools.

lesion_types_studied.details: Explicitly listed as a lesion type in the ISIC-2018 and HAM10000 datasets. The study highlights its importance for early diagnosis and notes that the model achieved strong predictive power in melanoma recognition. It is also grouped as a malignant class in the binary classification task (malignant vs. benign).
Explicitly listed as a lesion type in the ISIC-2018 and HAM10000 datasets. Included in the balanced split of the dataset to reflect real-world distribution.
Explicitly listed as a lesion type in the ISIC-2018 and HAM10000 datasets. Included in the balanced split of the dataset.

Referenced as 'non-melanoma classes' in the context of feature discrimination (PCA visualization) and 'benign lesions' in the context of the SVM's two-level decision strategy distinguishing malignant from benign. The abstract mentions 'other types' of skin lesions included in the datasets.

all_task_types.details: The paper uses YOLOv5 for real-time skin lesion localization and detection. The model predicts bounding boxes for lesions, with performance evaluated using Intersection over Union (IoU) and frames per second (FPS). The abstract and introduction explicitly state the model complements YOLOv5's 'real-time lesion localization ability'.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, etc.) and Malignant vs. Benign status
all_task_types.details: The paper employs a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for 'post-classification' of the localized lesions. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic classes (melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) and distinguishing between malignant and benign lesions. Performance is reported using classification metrics: accuracy (96.8%), F1-score (0.93), AUC (0.981), precision, and recall.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018

use.training: Used for training the YOLOv5-SVM hybrid model with a 70% split of the combined dataset.
use.validation: Used for validation with a 15% split of the combined dataset.
use.testing: Used for testing with a 15% split of the combined dataset.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset (part of the ISIC databases) used for skin lesion detection, containing various lesion types including melanoma, nevus, and basal cell carcinoma.

use.training: Used for training the YOLOv5-SVM hybrid model with a 70% split of the combined dataset.
use.validation: Used for validation with a 15% split of the combined dataset.
use.testing: Used for testing with a 15% split of the combined dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance, specifically assessing predictive power for melanoma recognition and distinguishing between malignant and benign lesions.
To illustrate the separation between malignant and benign classes and calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC).
To support model stability across varying thresholds and calculate Average Precision (AP).
To visualize feature separation and clustering between melanoma and non-melanoma classes in the final-layer embeddings.
To examine the importance of individual components (SVM classifier, YOLOv5 backbone, preprocessing steps) by measuring performance drops (e.g., accuracy reduction of ~3.2% without SVM).
To compare the proposed YOLOv5-SVM model against baseline models (YOLOv5, CNN, ResNet) using accuracy, F1-score, and IoU metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC, IoU) and ablation studies without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2018 and HAM10000 (Combined).description: Datasets used for training (70%), validation (15%), and testing (15%). Images resized to 640x640 pixels.
ISIC-2018 and HAM10000 (Combined).notes: The YOLOv5-SVM model outperformed all baseline models. Removing the SVM classifier reduced accuracy by ~3.2%. The ROC curve indicated an AUC of 1.000 for malignant vs benign separation in visualizations, while the abstract/text reports 0.981. The model maintained real-time inference speeds.

architecture_used.pretraining: YOLOv5 backbone pretrained on COCO dataset, then fine-tuned on skin lesion images.

architecture_details.svm_strategy: Two-level decision strategy for binary classification (malignant vs. benign)
architecture_details.pipeline: Two-stage pipeline: YOLOv5 for real-time lesion localization (bounding boxes) followed by SVM post-classification on cropped Regions of Interest (ROIs)

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (standard YOLOv5 training implied)
training_details.preprocessing: Contrast enhancement, histogram equalization, data augmentation, normalization

classification_or_output_head.function: Post-classification of localized lesions to distinguish between malignant and benign classes and specific lesion types (melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation (rotation, flipping along the x-axis, zooming)

The dataset was balanced to ensure representative cases for all applicable lesion categories, with a stratified split of 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% testing to preserve class balance.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid YOLOv5-SVM architecture achieves high diagnostic precision, with a classification accuracy of 96.8%, an F1-score of 0.93, an AUC of 0.981, and an IoU of 0.86 on the ISIC-2018 and HAM10000 datasets.
Integrating an SVM post-classifier with YOLOv5 significantly reduces false positives and enhances specificity in melanoma detection compared to standalone YOLOv5, ResNet, and standard CNN baselines.
The model maintains real-time inference speeds (~25 FPS on RTX 3060 and ~38 FPS on RTX 3090), making it suitable for deployment in tele-dermatology and mobile healthcare applications.

Ablation studies confirm that the SVM component is critical for performance, as its removal reduced accuracy by approximately 3.2%, while preprocessing steps like histogram equalization and data augmentation are essential for robustness and AUC.

The two-stage pipeline effectively addresses limitations of end-to-end deep learning models, such as overfitting and poor interpretability, by leveraging YOLOv5 for localization and SVM for detailed binary classification.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalizability across diverse skin tones, rare lesion types, and real-world clinical variability (e.g., noise, blurriness, low illumination).

Future work is identified to include domain adaptation for demographic diversity, incorporation of multimodal patient metadata (age, gender, medical history), and validation through prospective clinical trials prior to clinical integration.
important_remarks: The study relies on retrospective data from the ISIC 2018 and HAM10000 datasets without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability to diverse real-world populations.
The paper explicitly acknowledges limitations regarding generalizability across diverse skin tones and rare lesion types, noting that current models may struggle with demographic bias.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons are based on descriptive metrics and ablation studies.
The model was trained on a balanced dataset (70% train, 15% val, 15% test) to mitigate class imbalance, which may not reflect the true prevalence of lesions in clinical practice.
The research is published in a conference proceeding (HSWTech 2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.
The study does not include prospective clinical trials or validation in resource-poor settings, which are critical for assessing real-world clinical utility and deployment safety.
While the model claims real-time inference (~25-38 FPS), the computational requirements (NVIDIA RTX 3090) may limit deployment on standard mobile or edge devices without optimization.

The hybrid architecture's reliance on YOLOv5 for localization means that detection errors (false positives/negatives in bounding boxes) will directly impact the SVM classifier's input quality, creating a cascading error risk.
